food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — marinated beef tripe

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef tripe food product class; no specific entry for Beijing Hutong-style marinated preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers beef stomach/tripe as a category. No specific code for the marinated Beijing-style preparation exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for marinated beef tripe as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with sesame paste and chilli oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Beijing Hutong-style Marinated Beef Tripe is approximately 200 g, served as a cold appetiser or side dish at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Beef tripe, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13354)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef tripe (SR Legacy). Adjustments applied for the marinated preparation: sodium increased to reflect soy sauce and seasoning additions (~600 mg/100 g estimated); sesame paste contribution adds approximately 2 g fat and 1 g carbohydrate per 100 g of finished dish. Energy, protein, and fat values are consistent with cooked beef tripe with moderate sauce additions. This is an adapted estimate; no exact match for Beijing Hutong-style marinated beef tripe exists in an approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked offal dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing Hutong-style marinated beef tripe. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked offal and meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The dish contains approximately 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from sesame paste and soy sauce; the low carbohydrate content and high protein content strongly predict a low GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 5.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.8, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 610 mg per 100 g (approximately 1220 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting a reduced-soy-sauce preparation may lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beijing Hutong-style Marinated Beef Tripe is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g) and estimated low glycemic index (~32) result in a minimal glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per 200 g serving; however, sodium content should be monitored as hypertension is a common comorbidity in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content and high sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its cholesterol content (~95 mg/100 g) and high sodium from the soy sauce marinade; the sesame paste contributes beneficial unsaturated fats, but overall sodium intake should be carefully managed to avoid adverse blood pressure effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing Hutong-style Marinated Beef Tripe (北京胡同拌牛肚, Běijīng Hútòng Bàn Niú Dǔ) is a traditional cold appetiser originating from the hutong alleyway food culture of Beijing, China. The dish is prepared by blanching cleaned beef tripe — typically honeycomb or book tripe — until tender, then tossing it in a bold marinade of sesame paste, chilli oil, black or rice vinegar, soy sauce, minced garlic, and toasted sesame seeds, finished with spring onion and fresh coriander. The result is a dish that is simultaneously sour, spicy, nutty, and refreshing (酸辣爽口), with a firm yet slightly chewy texture characteristic of well-prepared tripe. Culturally, this dish represents the robust street-food and home-cooking tradition of Beijing's hutong neighbourhoods, where offal preparations have been prized for centuries as economical and flavourful. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.8 g/100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g/100 g), giving it a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 32 and a negligible glycemic load per serving. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, zinc, and iron from the beef tripe. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~610 mg/100 g) from the soy sauce and seasoning marinade, making it a dish that individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation. For individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile make it a relatively favourable protein-rich option. The dish contains sesame (allergen) and soy (allergen via soy sauce) and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Beijing Hutong-style Marinated Beef Tripe exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef tripe (SR Legacy) with adjustments for the marinade components. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked offal dishes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked beef tripe with estimated adjustments for soy sauce, sesame paste, chilli oil, and vinegar marinade; no exact approved-source match exists for this specific preparation. 2. Sodium is estimated at 610 mg/100 g to reflect the soy sauce and seasoning marinade; this is a key clinical variable and may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe. 8. Halal status is marked false as this dish is served at a general Chinese restaurant and halal certification cannot be confirmed from the source data. 9. The dish contains sesame and soy allergens. 10. This record was generated from a field survey data seed record (GD000841) served by See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 - Singapore Level 1.
